figures.caption: Figure 1. Compared with the direct predictions of MaskCLIP, the segmentation maps generated by clustering patch features from CLIP or DINOv3 exhibit superior spatial consistency.
Table 2. Proposed method is a plug-and-play module that can be seamlessly integrated into existing OVS methods.
Figure 6. Qualitative comparison of different OVSS methods on the OpenEarthMap (land cover mapping) datasets.
Figure 7. Qualitative comparison of different OVSS methods on the VDD (UAV aerial scene) datasets.

Figure 4. Qualitative comparison of different OVSS methods on the OpenEarthMap (land cover mapping), VDD (UAV aerial scene), and WBI-SI (water body segmentation) datasets. GT denotes the ground truth.

Table 3. Quantitative comparison on the open-vocabulary single-class remote sensing extraction task, measured by Foreground IoU. Bold and underlined values indicate the best and second-best performance, respectively.
Table 8. Performance comparison with different λ values across multiple datasets.
Table 9. Performance comparison on multiple datasets with varying λ.

Figure 2. Overview of the proposed Context-Aware Inference (ConInfer) framework. ConInfer performs joint, training-free inference by integrating semantic priors from CLIP with structural regularization from DINO. This formulation enables globally consistent openvocabulary segmentation without any retraining.
Table 5. The prompt class name of the evaluation datasets. indicates multiple prompt vocabularies for one class.
Figure 5. Convergence analysis was conducted on 8 datasets. During the iterative process, the loss rapidly converged and subsequently remained stable.
Figure 8. Qualitative comparison of different OVSS methods on the WBI-SI (water body segmentation) datasets.
